content.full_text:
Fnrn Por-rrrcAl PnecrrcEs C oMNrrssroN
-128 J Street . Suite 620 o Sacramento. CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 o Fax (916) 322-0886
June26,2009
Stephen L. Hartsell.
General Counsel
North Coast Schools Legal Consortium
901 Myrtle Avenue
Eureka, Califomia 95501
RE: Your Request for Informal Assistance
Our File No. I-09-132
Dear Mr. Hartsell
This letter responds to your request for advice on behalf of the Femdale Unified
School District regarding the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Political Reform Act
(the "Act").' This letter is based on the facts presented; the Fair Political Practices
Commission (the "Commission") does not act as a finder of fact when it renders
assistance. (ln re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71.)
Additionally, our advice is limited to obligations arising under the Act. We do
not address the applicability, if any, of other conflict-of-interest laws such as common
law conflict of interest or Government Code Section 1090. Because you seek general
information, we are treating your request as one for informal assistance.z
QUESTTON
Do three trustees of the Ferndale Unified School District (the "District") have a
conflict of interest under the Act by participating in a governmental decision regarding a
claim against the District brought by the superintendant when the three trustees are
alleged in the claim to be among the employees of the District to have breached their
duties toward the superintendant?
I Government Code sections 81000 - gl}l4. Commission regulations appear at Title 2, sections
I 8 109- I 8997, of the California Code of Regulations.
2 Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or
formal written advice. (Section 831l4; regulation 18329(cX3), copy enclosed).
{:b


File No. I-09-132
Page No. 2
CONCLUSION
The Act only concems conflicts of interest with respect to financial interests.
Unless the governmental decision will have a reasonably foreseeable material financial
effect on any of the trustees' economic interests as discussed herein, they do not have a
conflict of interest under the Act by participating in any governmental decision to accept,
reject, or compromise the claim.' Based on the facts presented, the claim will not have a
financial effect on these trustees' economic interests. Therefore, they would have no
conflict of interest under the Act if they participate in governmental decisions regarding
the claim.
FACTS
A claim for damages dated May 13, 2009,has been submitted to the District by its
superintendant, Sam Garamendi. The claim alleges that three members of the District's
board of trustees engaged in conduct that violated his rights. There is nothing in the
claim indicating an intention to name any of those trustees as individual defendants in
any action that may later be brought against the District.a
Govemment Code Section9l2.6 identifies four ways that the board of trustees
may act on this claim. It may: (1) reject the claim; (2) allow the claim; (3) allow it in the
amount justly due and reject the balance; and (4) compromise the claim. Government
Code Section9l2.4(c) provides that if the board takes no action on the claim within 45
days of its presentation, it is deemed to have been rejected. There are no facts indicating
that the members would ever have to pay the claim personally. You seek advice as to
whether the Act allows the three trustees identified in the claim to participate in a
decision by the board of trustees regarding the claim.
ANALYSIS
PotrNrr^lr, CoNrr,rcr oF INTEREsT
The Act's conflict-of-interest provisions ensure that public officials will "perform
their duties in an impartial manner, free from bias caused by their own financial interests
or the financial interests of persons who have supported them." (Section 81001(b).)
Section 87100 prohibits any public official from making, participating in making, or
otherwise using his or her official position to influence a govemmental decision in which
the official has a financial interest.
The Commission has adopted an eight-step standard analysis for deciding whether
an official has a disqualifying conflict of interest. (Regulation 18700(b).) The general
rule, however, is that a conflict of interest exists whenever a public official makes a
r The three trustees are Susan Petersen, Danette Lentz. and Joanne Walters.
a You have attached a copy ofthe claim for damages. It does not indicate any action against any
of the individuals mentioned


File No. I-09-132
Page No. 3
govemmental decision that has a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on one
or more of his or her financial interests.
SrBps I &2t Anr rnB Tnnrr Tnusrnrs Punr,rc Orrrcr.rr,s MAKTNG,
PanrrcplrrNc IN MAKTNG, oRINFLUENCTNG A GovERNMENTAL DncrsroN?
Each of the named trustees is a public official under the Act. (Section 82048.)
Consequently, they may not make, participate in making, or otherwise use their official
position to influence any decisions that will have a reasonably foreseeable material
financial effect on any of their economic interests. They will be called upon to consider a
claim for damages against the District. Therefore, they will be making, participating in
making, or otherwise using his official position to influence a governmental decision.
srrp 3: Do rnr THneB Ntunn TRusrrns H,lvB A PoTENTTALLv Drsqu.lr,rrvmc
EcoNo*rrc lNrrnrsr?
A public official has a financial interest in a decision within the meaning of
Section 87103 if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material
financial effect, distinguishable from its effect on the public generally, on the official, a
member of his or her immediate family, or on any one of five enumerated economic
interests, including:
An economic interest in a business entity in which he or she has a direct or
indirect investment of $2,000 or more (Section 87103(a); Regulation
18703.1(a)); or in which he or she is a director, officer, partner, trustee,
employee, or holds any position of management. (Section 87103(d);
Regulation I 8703. 1(b).)
a
a
An economic interest in real property in which he or she has a direct or
indirect interest of $2,000 or more. (Section 87103(b); Regulation
18703.2.)
An economic interest in any source of income, including promised
income, aggregating $500 or more within 12 months prior to the decision.
(Section 87103(c); Regulation 18703.3.)
An economic interest in any source of gifts to him or her if the gifts
aggregate to $420 or more within 12 months prior to the decision. (Section
87 103(e); Regulation 18703.4.)
An economic interest in his or her personal finances, including those of his
or her immediate family -- this is the "personal financial effects" rule.
(Section 87103; Regulation 18703.5.)
o


File No. I-09-132
Page No.4
The facts indicate that the claim is against the District, not individual district
officers or employees, and there is nothing to indicate that any consequence of this
decision will financially affect any of the economic interest identified above with respect
to any of the named trustees. Therefore, so long as the govemmental decision will not
have a reasonably foreseeable financial effect on any of the individual trustees economic
interests, the trustees may participate in the decision, and our analysis ends here.
lf you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 3zz-s66o.
Sincerely,
Scott Hallabrin
Counsel
By:
Lenkeit
Senior Counsel, Legal Division
wJLjgl
Enclosure
